source_label: Bitcoin Optech — Topics on Initial Block Download
excerpt: The chainstate is the authoritative per-node view of all currently-unspent outputs. Historical block data is necessary for re-validation and for serving peers but not for validating new blocks once the chainstate is in place.
body:
Source excerpt:
The chainstate is the authoritative per-node view of all currently-unspent outputs. Historical block data is necessary for re-validation and for serving peers but not for validating new blocks once the chainstate is in place.

---

Cited by leaf "IBD, chainstate, indexing options":
When Bitcoin Core starts on a fresh data directory, it performs *initial block download* (IBD): connecting to peers, downloading every block from genesis to the chain tip, validating each, and updating the chainstate. The current chain is over a million blocks deep and ~600 GB of raw block data on disk; IBD takes anywhere from hours to days depending on disk, CPU, and network. *Headers-first* sync (introduced in 0.10) speeds this up by downloading the entire header chain first (~80 MB), validating proof of work, then fetching block bodies in parallel from many peers [1].

During IBD, *script validation* is the dominant CPU cost. Since 0.14, Core skips signature checks for blocks below an *assumevalid* hash (a recent block hash committed to in the source code) — these blocks have already been buried under so much proof of work that re-checking signatures is computationally wasteful, while the proof-of-work check itself remains. This shortcut is opt-out (`-assumevalid=0`) for paranoid operators [1]. *Assumeutxo* (added in 0.26) goes further: a node can start from a snapshot of the chainstate at a known height, skipping IBD entirely for the early chain, and validate normally from the snapshot point forward.

The *chainstate* is the per-node database of all currently-unspent transaction outputs (UTXOs). It is what a node actually consults to validate new blocks; the historical block files are largely just for serving peers and for re-validating from scratch. The chainstate is a few gigabytes (and growing slowly), backed by LevelDB, and lives in `chainstate/` under the datadir [2].

*Pruning* (`-prune=N`) discards old block and undo files once their effects on the chainstate have been applied, keeping only the most recent N MB of block data on disk. A pruned node can still validate every new block (the chainstate has everything it needs) but cannot serve historical blocks to peers and cannot re-rescan a wallet for old transactions. This is the configuration most well-resourced individuals run.

*Indexing options* are opt-in additional databases the node can maintain alongside the chainstate: `txindex` (every transaction by txid), `coinstatsindex` (cumulative supply / UTXO count per block), `blockfilterindex` (BIP-158 compact filters for light-client privacy). Each costs disk and indexing time but unlocks specific use cases — `txindex` for block explorers, `blockfilterindex` for serving Neutrino-style light wallets [3].

---

Cited by leaf "Running your own full node":
A full node downloads every block since the genesis block, validates every transaction in every block against the consensus rules, and keeps a current view of the UTXO set. What this gets you operationally: your wallet queries your own node for balance and history, you broadcast transactions to your own peer-to-peer network connection, and you don't have to trust any third party's view of "what is the current state of Bitcoin" [1]. The cost is some disk and a one-time multi-day initial sync.

Resource requirements as of 2026: ~700 GB of disk if you keep the full chain, ~10 GB if you run pruned (which keeps consensus enforcement but discards old block data), 4–8 GB of RAM, and a sustained internet connection that can handle the upload bandwidth peers will draw from you (a few hundred GB/month is typical). Initial block download is the painful part — it takes one to several days on consumer hardware depending on CPU, disk, and which validation shortcuts (assumeutxo, assumevalid) you accept [2].

What a full node does not do: it does not by itself give you privacy (your wallet still has to query the node in a way that doesn't leak your addresses to your ISP); it does not pay you for being run; it does not give you a vote in any governance process. What it does do, in addition to the trust-minimized wallet experience, is contribute to the network's resilience by serving blocks to other peers and by being one more independent enforcement point for the consensus rules [3]. The honest framing: running a node is an operational discipline, not a hobby with a payoff curve. The payoff is a different relationship to your own money.
